text: A brain tumor is a mass of unnecessary cells growing in the brain or central spine canal. There are two basic kinds of brain tumors – primary brain tumors and metastatic brain tumors. Primary brain tumors start and tend to stay in the brain. Metastatic brain tumors begin as cancer elsewhere in the body and spread to the brain. Each year more than 68,470 people in the United States are diagnosed with a primary brain tumor and more than twice that number are diagnosed with a metastatic tumor.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: A tumor that starts in the brain is a primary brain tumor. Glioblastoma multiforme, astrocytoma, medulloblastoma and ependymoma are examples of primary brain tumors. Primary brain tumors are grouped into benign tumors and malignant tumors. Benign brain tumors consist of very slow-growing cells, usually has distinct borders and rarely spreads. When viewed under a microscope, these cells have an almost normal appearance. Surgery alone might be an effective treatment for this type of tumor.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: When viewed under a microscope, these cells have an almost normal appearance. Surgery alone might be an effective treatment for this type of tumor. Malignant brain tumors are usually rapid-growing, invasive and life-threatening. Malignant brain tumors are sometimes called brain cancer. However, since primary brain tumors rarely spread outside the brain and spinal cord, they do not exactly fit the general definition of cancer.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text:
Tumors are graded to facilitate communication, plan treatment and predict outcomes. The grade of a tumor indicates its degree of malignancy. Using the WHO grading system:

Grade I tumors are the least malignant and are usually associated with long-term survival. The tumors grow slowly and have an almost normal appearance when viewed through a microscope. Grade II tumors are relatively slow-growing and have a slightly abnormal microscopic appearance.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: The tumors grow slowly and have an almost normal appearance when viewed through a microscope. Grade II tumors are relatively slow-growing and have a slightly abnormal microscopic appearance. Some can spread into nearby normal tissue and recur. Grade III tumors are by definition malignant. The cells are actively reproducing abnormal cells which grow into nearby normal brain tissue. These tumors tend to recur, often as a higher grade. Grade IV tumors are the most malignant.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: The cells are actively reproducing abnormal cells which grow into nearby normal brain tissue. These tumors tend to recur, often as a higher grade. Grade IV tumors are the most malignant. They reproduce rapidly, can have a bizarre appearance under the microscope and easily grow into surrounding normal brain tissue. Glioblastoma is the most common example of a grade IV tumor.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: The brain is a soft mass of supportive tissues and nerve cells connected to the spinal cord. The brain and spinal cord together form the central nervous system (CNS). The central nervous system is the core of our existence.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: The brain and spinal cord together form the central nervous system (CNS). The central nervous system is the core of our existence. It controls our personality – thoughts, memory, intelligence, speech, understanding and emotions; our senses – vision, hearing, taste, smell and touch; our basic body functions – breathing, heart beat and blood pressure; and how we function in our environment – movement, balance and coordination.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text:
The brain consists of several major parts:

**Cerebrum/Cerebral Hemispheres**: The largest area of the brain, consisting of the right and left cerebral hemispheres. Each hemisphere is comprised of four sections called lobes: frontal, parietal, temporal and occipital. **Cerebellum**: The second largest area of the brain, located in the back of the head between the cerebrum and the brain stem. It controls balance and coordination.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: **Cerebellum**: The second largest area of the brain, located in the back of the head between the cerebrum and the brain stem. It controls balance and coordination. **Brain Stem**: The bottom-most portion of the brain that connects the cerebrum with the spinal cord. It contains the midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Astrocytomas are tumors that arise from astrocytes – cells that make up the supportive tissue of the brain. These tumors are graded by the pathologist to indicate how normal or abnormal the cells look under the microscope. The WHO system grades astrocytomas on a scale from I to IV. Grade I tumors include pilocytic astrocytomas, which are usually localized tumors that are often cured through surgical removal.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Grade I tumors include pilocytic astrocytomas, which are usually localized tumors that are often cured through surgical removal. Grade II to IV tumors have increasing degrees of malignancy and although surgery is beneficial, it is not curative for these tumors.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: 'Grade IV astrocytoma,' 'glioblastoma' and 'GBM' are all names for the same tumor. This tumor represents about 16% of all primary brain tumors and about 60–75% of all astrocytomas. They increase in frequency with age and affect more men than women. Glioblastomas are generally found in the cerebral hemispheres but can be found anywhere in the brain or spinal cord. The most common symptoms are usually due to increased pressure in the brain and can include headache, nausea, vomiting and drowsiness.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: The most common symptoms are usually due to increased pressure in the brain and can include headache, nausea, vomiting and drowsiness. Treatment typically includes surgery followed by radiation therapy and chemotherapy. The most commonly used chemotherapy drug in adults is temozolomide.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Meningiomas arise from the arachnoid mater – one of the layers of the meninges (the lining of the brain). Meningiomas represent about 38% of all primary brain tumors and occur most frequently in middle-aged women. The majority of meningiomas are benign, grade I, slow-growing tumors which are localized and non-infiltrating. They are most often located between the cerebral hemispheres, within the meninges, at the base of the skull, and in the back, lower part of the brain.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: They are most often located between the cerebral hemispheres, within the meninges, at the base of the skull, and in the back, lower part of the brain. The standard treatment is surgery to remove the tumor. Total removal appears critical for long-term tumor control. Radiation therapy or radiosurgery might be used if the tumor is not entirely removed.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Medulloblastomas represent about 13% of brain tumors in children under age 14 and about 3% in adults. These tumors are always located in the cerebellum. Medulloblastoma is a fast-growing, high-grade tumor which frequently spreads to other parts of the central nervous system. Treatment consists of surgical removal of as much tumor as possible, radiation and chemotherapy.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Medulloblastoma is a fast-growing, high-grade tumor which frequently spreads to other parts of the central nervous system. Treatment consists of surgical removal of as much tumor as possible, radiation and chemotherapy. For older children and adults without evidence of tumor spreading and those for whom most tumor has been removed, radiation to the tumor area followed by lower dose radiation to the entire brain and spinal cord is standard. Chemotherapy generally follows radiation therapy.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Ependymomas may originate from ependymal cells (which line the ventricles of the brain and the center of the spinal cord) or from radial glial cells. These are relatively rare tumors, accounting for 1–2% of all primary tumors. They are divided into four major types: subependymomas (grade I), myxopapillary ependymomas and ependymomas (grade II) and anaplastic ependymomas (grade III). The first step in treatment is surgery to remove as much tumor as possible.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: They are divided into four major types: subependymomas (grade I), myxopapillary ependymomas and ependymomas (grade II) and anaplastic ependymomas (grade III). The first step in treatment is surgery to remove as much tumor as possible. Radiation therapy is usually recommended for older children and adults following surgery if all visible tumor wasn't removed.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Pituitary tumors represent 13% of all primary brain tumors. The pituitary gland produces several hormones including prolactin, corticotropin and growth hormone. A tumor in this area may change the amount of hormones made by the pituitary gland. Hormone disturbances can cause water balance problems, abnormal growth, sleep disturbances and emotional changes. Treatment depends on the type of tumor and symptoms. Options may include surgery, radiation therapy, or medication to control hormone production.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Causes and risk factors can be environmental, such as being exposed to poisonous substances, or genetic, such as being born with a gene mutation. Unfortunately, no risk factor accounting for the majority of brain tumors has been identified, even though many environmental and genetic factors have been and are currently being studied. Of the long list of factors studied, only exposure to ionizing radiation has been consistently associated with an increased risk for developing a brain tumor.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Only 5–10% of all cancer is actually inherited from one generation to another in a family (hereditary).

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text:
Only 5–10% of all cancer is actually inherited from one generation to another in a family (hereditary). There are a few rare, inherited genetic syndromes that involve brain tumors, including:
- Neurofibromatosis 1 (NF1 gene)
- Neurofibromatosis 2 (NF2 gene)
- Turcots (APC gene)
- Gorlins (PTCH gene)
- Tuberous Sclerosis (TSC1 and TSC2 genes)
- Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53 gene)

The vast majority of genetic risk factors are not inherited at birth but actually accumulate over time as we age (also called somatic or acquired).

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text:
With a limited amount of space in the skull, the growth of anything that doesn't belong there can change the way the brain works. Tumors can cause direct damage to brain tissue, a shifting of the brain as the tumor grows or pressure on the brain. The most common tumor locations and their effects:

**Frontal Tumors**: Mood swings, personality changes, difficulty with problem solving, short-term memory issues, difficulty expressing thoughts in words.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text:
The most common tumor locations and their effects:

**Frontal Tumors**: Mood swings, personality changes, difficulty with problem solving, short-term memory issues, difficulty expressing thoughts in words. **Parietal Tumors**: Decreased awareness of sensation, difficulty recognizing body position or parts, confusion of left and right sides. **Temporal Tumors**: Can cause a dreamy 'déjà vu' state, disturbed sense of time, difficulty hearing and understanding sounds, behavior changes.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: **Temporal Tumors**: Can cause a dreamy 'déjà vu' state, disturbed sense of time, difficulty hearing and understanding sounds, behavior changes. **Occipital Tumors**: Visual disturbances, loss of vision in visual field, difficulty recognizing what is seen. **Cerebellar-Pontine Angle Tumors**: Ringing in ears, one-sided hearing loss, dizziness, facial weakness.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text:
Common symptoms that may indicate a brain tumor include:
- Headaches (especially new or different pattern)
- Seizures
- Nausea or vomiting
- Vision or hearing problems
- Balance and coordination difficulties
- Personality or behavioral changes
- Memory problems
- Weakness or numbness

However, these symptoms can be caused by many conditions. Only medical professionals can diagnose a brain tumor through proper examination and imaging tests.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: A seizure is an episode of abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Between 25 and 40% of people diagnosed with a brain tumor will have a seizure at some time during their illness. During normal brain activity, nerve cells communicate through carefully controlled electric-like signals. If something interferes with those signals and they become more intense, a seizure results. Most seizures can be controlled with medications called antiepileptic drugs (AEDs).

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: If something interferes with those signals and they become more intense, a seizure results. Most seizures can be controlled with medications called antiepileptic drugs (AEDs). Surgery or a ketogenic diet are also sometimes used to help treat ongoing seizures.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text:
There are two primary types of seizures:

**Partial Seizures**: Include simple partial seizures (jerking, twitching, sensations) and complex partial seizures (loss of consciousness, uncontrolled movements). **Generalized Seizures**: Include absence seizures (brief delays in consciousness), atonic seizures (sudden limpness), myoclonic seizures (muscle twitches), and tonic-clonic/grand mal seizures (loss of consciousness with muscle contractions).

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: **Generalized Seizures**: Include absence seizures (brief delays in consciousness), atonic seizures (sudden limpness), myoclonic seizures (muscle twitches), and tonic-clonic/grand mal seizures (loss of consciousness with muscle contractions). The type of seizure depends on which area of the brain is experiencing the abnormal electrical signals.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Children are not smaller versions of adults. Their bodies and brains are still developing. Their needs are different. Their tumors are different too. Brain tumors are the most common form of solid tumors among children under age 15 and represent about 20% of all childhood cancers. The most common childhood brain tumors are astrocytoma, medulloblastoma and ependymoma.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Brain tumors are the most common form of solid tumors among children under age 15 and represent about 20% of all childhood cancers. The most common childhood brain tumors are astrocytoma, medulloblastoma and ependymoma. Pediatric brain tumors require specialized care that is most often provided by a team of specialists at a large medical center or pediatric hospital.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: Treatment decisions can have unintended consequences known as 'late effects.' Because children can live for many decades post-treatment, it's essential that parents and caregivers be aware of potential late effects. Late effects vary based on age, tumor type and location, treatment type and duration. Some effects may be apparent almost immediately, but some may not emerge until years after treatment.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text:
Some effects may be apparent almost immediately, but some may not emerge until years after treatment. Common complications include:
- Physical disabilities (weakness, diminished coordination)
- Learning disabilities (memory, attention, comprehension issues)
- Behavioral changes and emotional issues
- Hearing and vision problems
- Seizures
- Hormonal problems
- Possibility of developing secondary cancers

Lifetime monitoring by medical teams versed in late effects is essential.

metadata.source: American Brain Tumor Association - About Brain Tumors Primer

text: ⚠️ MEDICAL DISCLAIMER: This system does not provide medical advice. It is an educational resource based on the American Brain Tumor Association's public materials. Always consult qualified healthcare professionals for medical decisions, diagnosis, and treatment. If you are experiencing a medical emergency, call emergency services immediately.
